RT @unity: We’re excited to introduce the Unity CLI, our new terminal-native path for connecting coding agents, CI pipelines, and custom to…

Kigi is The first CLI with graph engineering built in

June was all about loop engineering: wrap an agent in a loop and let it run until the job is done. I added a /graph command to Kigi, the open-source CLI I maintain, and took the next step: a loop defines one agent's behavior, a graph defines how many agents are organized.

The principle in one line: lock the model's nondeterminism inside nodes, keep every edge between nodes scheduled by deterministic code. Research (the SGH paper) and engineering (LangGraph, Mastra) both converged on this in 2026; Kigi ships it as a single CLI command.

Five layers:

Decompose. You give one objective. A planner subagent splits it into a dependency DAG as JSON. Static validation runs first: cycles, unknown dependencies, duplicate nodes all bounce back for a rewrite. Nothing executes before validation passes.

Execute. Each node is one autonomous loop: work, get verified, retry with the reviewer's complaints, capped rounds (default 3). Independent nodes run in parallel (default 3), each worker in its own git worktree, merged back three-way in order. A merge conflict fails that node and blocks its dependents; other chains keep going.

Verify. Every node gets an adversarial verifier whose job is to refute the work; no "achieved", no pass. After the whole graph, a terminal node re-checks the full objective, catching the case where every node passed but the whole didn't.

Evolve. When a worker hits necessary out-of-scope work, it reports one line: DISCOVERED:. Replanning appends nodes without touching old ones, bumps the plan version, freezes the old plan as an immutable baseline. An optimizer at plan boundaries deletes false dependencies and splits or merges nodes to restore parallelism.

State. The graph lives in .kigi/graph.jsonl inside your repo. New session, new machine: /graph resume continues where it stopped. A file lock keeps a single writer.

All open source. Search Kigi-CLI on GitHub, install, type /graph plus one objective, and watch it decompose and finish the job.

这次统一测试 opus 4.8、GPT-5.5、Kimi K2.6、MiniMax M3、GLM-5.1、DeepSeek V4 Pro。平台统一用 https://t.co/dz1ATC4EZv，同一套 prompt、同一套 PRD、同一套 AGENTS.md，全部 xhigh 模式，全部用 /goal 跑到 AI 自己认为完成为止。代码也会全部开源在我的 GitHub：ZacharyZhang-NY/ModelTest2026

第一轮做的是通用团队业务运营 SaaS，覆盖登录、工作区、权限、客户、项目、任务、笔记、看板、报表、审计日志、设置页。

这一轮整体完成度都挺高，功能基本都能做出来，真正拉开差距的是 UI 审美。DeepSeek 和 MiniMax 比较惊喜。DeepSeek 风格和其他家明显不同，虽然还有一些典型的“方格边框+颜色条”AI味，但整体观感不错。MiniMax 的 AI 味儿比较少，也没有 opus 那种看板里大框套小框再套小小框的问题。GLM 和 Kimi 中规中矩。GPT-5.5 前端依然很灾难，甚至出现按钮文字超框的问题。

第一轮排名：opus = deepseek = minimax > kimi > GLM > GPT。

第二轮是纯 UI 复刻。我给每个模型同一个视频 demo 网站首页，让它们根据视频里的动效复刻网站。这里差距非常大。GLM 基本不可用；DeepSeek 没有动效；GPT 有动效，但偏移很明显；Kimi 做了量子点阵特效，完成度很高；MiniMax M3 是最大惊喜，唯一做了开场动画，而且动画和原视频有相似结构，完成度最高。opus 和 DeepSeek 类似，动效和 hero 背景都没跟上。

第二轮排名：MiniMax > Kimi > GPT > OPUS = DeepSeek >>> GLM。

这轮最让我意外的是 MiniMax M3。视觉能力确实到了 T0 级别，和其他模型拉开了明显差距，长时任务能力也很猛。MiniMax 3.0 时代进步很明显，继续加油。
